high-ping.label: High Ping / Latency
high-ping.description: Experiencing high round-trip times when connecting to servers

questions.text: How are you connected to the network?
When does the high ping occur?

options.label: Only when others are using the network

questions.text: What is your approximate ping to common servers (e.g., google.com)?
Are you using a VPN?
How many devices are on your network?

wifi_interference.title: WiFi Interference
wifi_interference.description: Your WiFi signal is being disrupted by other wireless devices, neighboring networks, or physical obstacles. This causes inconsistent latency and packet loss.
solutions: Switch to the 5GHz band if your router supports dual-band
Change your WiFi channel to avoid overlap with neighbors (use a WiFi analyzer app)
Move closer to your router or remove physical obstacles
Avoid placing the router near microwaves, baby monitors, or cordless phones
Consider upgrading to a WiFi 6 router for better interference handling

isp_congestion.title: ISP Network Congestion
isp_congestion.description: Your Internet Service Provider's network is experiencing congestion, especially during peak usage hours. This is common in areas with shared bandwidth infrastructure like cable internet.
solutions: Test your speed at different times to confirm peak-hour congestion
Contact your ISP to report consistent performance issues
Consider upgrading your internet plan for higher priority traffic
Use a wired connection to eliminate local WiFi as a factor
Try changing your DNS server to a faster one (8.8.8.8 or 1.1.1.1)

routing_issue.title: Routing / Path Problem
routing_issue.description: Packets are taking an inefficient path to reach their destination, possibly due to suboptimal routing by your ISP or a misconfigured gateway.
solutions: Run a traceroute to identify where delays occur: tracert google.com (Windows) or traceroute google.com (Mac/Linux)
Check if double NAT is occurring (router behind router)
Restart your modem and router to refresh routing tables
Contact your ISP if traceroute shows high latency at their network hops
Try a different DNS server to rule out DNS-related delays

bandwidth_saturation.title: Bandwidth Saturation
bandwidth_saturation.description: Your network bandwidth is being fully utilized by multiple devices or applications, leaving insufficient capacity for your traffic.
solutions: Enable QoS (Quality of Service) on your router to prioritize important traffic
Identify bandwidth-heavy applications (streaming, downloads) and limit them
Schedule large downloads for off-peak hours
Upgrade your internet plan if bandwidth is consistently insufficient
Use a network monitor to identify which devices consume the most bandwidth

mtu_mismatch.title: MTU Mismatch
mtu_mismatch.description: The Maximum Transmission Unit size doesn't match between your device and the network, causing packet fragmentation and increased latency.
solutions: Test optimal MTU: ping google.com -f -l 1472 and reduce until no fragmentation
Set your network adapter's MTU to the optimal value found
Common optimal MTU values: 1500 (Ethernet), 1492 (PPPoE), 1400 (VPN)
Check your router settings for MTU configuration
Contact your ISP if MTU issues persist after local adjustments

vpn_overhead.title: VPN Encryption Overhead
vpn_overhead.description: Your VPN adds encryption overhead and routes traffic through remote servers, adding latency. The VPN server location and protocol also affect speed.
solutions: Connect to a VPN server geographically closer to you
Try a different VPN protocol (WireGuard is faster than OpenVPN)
Use split tunneling to route only necessary traffic through VPN
Disable VPN when low latency is critical (gaming, video calls)
Check if your VPN provider offers optimized servers for your use case

packet-loss.label: Packet Loss
packet-loss.description: Data packets are being dropped before reaching their destination

questions.text: How are you connected?
What percentage of packet loss are you seeing?
Is the packet loss consistent or intermittent?

wifi_signal_weak.title: Weak WiFi Signal
wifi_signal_weak.description: Your device is too far from the router or obstacles are blocking the signal, causing packets to be lost in transmission.
solutions: Move closer to your WiFi router
Remove physical obstructions between your device and the router
Add a WiFi extender or mesh system for better coverage
Update your network adapter drivers
Consider using a USB WiFi adapter with an external antenna

cable_fault.title: Faulty Network Cable
cable_fault.description: The Ethernet cable connecting your device may be damaged, poorly crimped, or too long, causing intermittent connectivity issues.
solutions: Replace the Ethernet cable with a known-good one
Check cable connections at both ends for secure fit
Inspect the cable for visible damage, kinks, or sharp bends
Use Cat5e or Cat6 cable for reliable gigabit connections
Keep cable runs under 100 meters (328 feet)

channel_congestion.title: WiFi Channel Congestion
channel_congestion.description: Too many nearby networks are using the same WiFi channel, causing interference and packet loss.
solutions: Use a WiFi analyzer app to find the least congested channel
Switch to the 5GHz band which has more available channels
Manually set your router to the optimal channel
Consider using DFS channels on 5GHz if supported
Upgrade to WiFi 6 which handles dense environments better

hardware_failure.title: Network Hardware Failure
hardware_failure.description: Your router, modem, or network adapter may be failing, causing consistent packet loss.
solutions: Restart your router and modem (power cycle for 30 seconds)
Check if the router is overheating and ensure ventilation
Update your router's firmware to the latest version
Test with a different device to isolate the problem
Consider replacing aging network equipment (5+ years old)

dns-failure.label: DNS Failure
dns-failure.description: Unable to resolve domain names to IP addresses

questions.text: Can you access websites by their IP address? (e.g., http://142.250.80.46 for Google)
Are all websites failing or just specific ones?
Have you recently changed any network settings?

dns_server_down.title: DNS Server Unreachable
dns_server_down.description: Your configured DNS server is down or unreachable. This prevents your computer from translating domain names to IP addresses.
solutions: Switch to a public DNS: Google (8.8.8.8) or Cloudflare (1.1.1.1)
Open network adapter settings and manually set DNS servers
Flush your DNS cache: ipconfig /flushdns (Windows) or sudo dscacheutil -flushcache (Mac)
Restart your router to refresh its DNS configuration
Check if your ISP's DNS servers are experiencing an outage

dns_config_wrong.title: DNS Configuration Error
dns_config_wrong.description: Your DNS settings are misconfigured — either the DNS server addresses are wrong or DHCP isn't providing correct DNS info.
solutions: Check your network adapter's DNS settings
Try setting DNS to automatic (via DHCP)
Manually configure DNS to 8.8.8.8 and 8.8.4.4
Verify your router's DHCP settings are providing correct DNS
Run 'nslookup google.com' to test DNS resolution

dns_cache_stale.title: Stale DNS Cache
dns_cache_stale.description: Your computer or router has cached outdated DNS records that no longer point to the correct IP addresses.
solutions: Flush DNS cache: ipconfig /flushdns (Windows)
Clear your browser's DNS cache (chrome://net-internals/#dns in Chrome)
Restart your router to clear its DNS cache
Try accessing the website in an incognito/private window
Wait for TTL expiration if a website recently changed servers

no_internet.title: No Internet Connectivity
no_internet.description: The issue isn't DNS-specific — you have no internet connectivity at all. DNS failures are a symptom of a larger connectivity problem.
solutions: Check if your router has internet connectivity (check router admin page)
Restart your modem and router
Check if your ISP is experiencing an outage
Verify all cables are securely connected
Try connecting a different device to confirm

slow-internet.label: Slow Internet Speed
slow-internet.description: Internet connection is significantly slower than expected

questions.text: What speed are you paying for vs. getting?

options.label: Getting less than 50% of paid speed
Speed varies widely throughout the day

questions.text: Connection type?
Is the slowness specific to certain websites or all traffic?

wifi_limitation.title: WiFi Speed Limitation
wifi_limitation.description: Your WiFi connection is the bottleneck. Older WiFi standards, distance from router, and interference can significantly reduce speeds.
solutions: Connect via Ethernet cable for maximum speed
Move closer to the router for a stronger signal
Use the 5GHz band for faster speeds (shorter range)
Update your WiFi adapter drivers
Upgrade to a WiFi 6 router if using older hardware

bandwidth_throttle.title: ISP Bandwidth Throttling
bandwidth_throttle.description: Your ISP may be intentionally slowing your connection, especially for certain types of traffic like streaming or P2P.
solutions: Run speed tests at different times and compare results
Try a VPN to see if speeds improve (bypasses traffic shaping)
Contact your ISP to inquire about throttling policies
Check if you've exceeded any data caps
Consider switching to an ISP that doesn't throttle

congestion.title: Network Congestion
congestion.description: Too many users on the same network segment or ISP node are consuming bandwidth simultaneously.
solutions: Test speeds during off-peak hours to confirm congestion
Enable QoS on your router
Limit the number of simultaneous connections
Report persistent congestion to your ISP
Consider a business-grade connection for guaranteed bandwidth

hardware_bottleneck.title: Hardware Bottleneck
hardware_bottleneck.description: Your router, modem, or network adapter may not support the speeds you're paying for.
solutions: Check your router's maximum throughput specification
Verify your Ethernet adapter supports Gigabit speeds
Update firmware on your router and modem
Replace any 10/100 Mbps equipment with Gigabit-capable devices
Check if your modem supports your ISP's DOCSIS version

server_side.title: Server-Side Limitation
server_side.description: The slowness is on the remote server's end, not your connection. The website or service you're accessing may be overloaded.
solutions: Check if other users report the same issue (downdetector.com)
Try accessing the service from a different network
Clear your browser cache and cookies
Try a different browser or incognito mode
Wait and retry later if the server is experiencing high load

websites-not-loading.label: Websites Not Loading
websites-not-loading.description: Web pages fail to load or show connection errors

questions.text: What error are you seeing?
Can you access any websites at all?
Do other devices on the same network have the same problem?

dns_issue.title: DNS Resolution Failure
dns_issue.description: Your device cannot translate domain names into IP addresses. This is the most common cause of websites not loading.
solutions: Change DNS to 8.8.8.8 (Google) or 1.1.1.1 (Cloudflare)
Flush DNS cache: ipconfig /flushdns
Restart your router and modem
Check network adapter DNS settings
Disable any DNS filtering software temporarily

firewall_block.title: Firewall or Security Software Blocking
firewall_block.description: Your firewall, antivirus, or security software is blocking web traffic on ports 80/443.
solutions: Temporarily disable your firewall to test
Check firewall rules for blocked ports (80, 443)
Add your browser to the firewall whitelist
Check if parental controls are blocking sites
Review antivirus web protection settings

cert_issue.title: SSL/TLS Certificate Problem
cert_issue.description: The website's security certificate is invalid, expired, or your system doesn't trust it.
solutions: Check your system clock — incorrect time causes certificate errors
Clear browser SSL state and cached certificates
Update your browser to the latest version
Check if the website's certificate has actually expired
Try accessing the HTTP version if available (not recommended for sensitive sites)

no_connectivity.title: No Internet Connectivity
no_connectivity.description: Your device has lost its internet connection entirely.
solutions: Check WiFi/Ethernet connection status
Restart your network adapter
Power cycle your router and modem
Run network troubleshooter in your OS settings
Contact your ISP if the problem persists

gaming-lag.label: Gaming Lag
gaming-lag.description: Experiencing lag, rubber-banding, or disconnections while gaming

questions.text: What type of lag are you experiencing?
Are you connected via WiFi or Ethernet?
Are other applications running in the background?

wifi_instability.title: WiFi Instability
wifi_instability.description: WiFi introduces variable latency (jitter) which is particularly problematic for real-time gaming. Even small fluctuations cause noticeable lag.
solutions: Switch to a wired Ethernet connection (most impactful fix)
If WiFi is mandatory, use 5GHz band and sit close to the router
Disable other WiFi devices during gaming sessions
Enable QoS on your router and prioritize gaming traffic
Use a powerline adapter as an alternative to WiFi

server_distance.title: Game Server Distance
server_distance.description: You're connecting to a game server that's geographically far from you, resulting in inherently high latency.
solutions: Select a game server in your region if the game allows it
Check the game's server browser for closest servers
Consider a gaming VPN that routes through optimized paths
Accept that some base latency is unavoidable for distant servers
Check if the game supports server migration or region selection

background_traffic.title: Background Network Traffic
background_traffic.description: Other applications or devices are consuming bandwidth, causing your game traffic to be delayed or dropped.
solutions: Close streaming services, downloads, and cloud syncing during gaming
Enable QoS on your router and prioritize gaming traffic
Schedule Windows updates for non-gaming hours
Ask others on your network to reduce heavy usage during your gaming time
Use Task Manager to identify and close bandwidth-heavy processes

isp_routing.title: ISP Routing Issue
isp_routing.description: Your ISP is routing your traffic through suboptimal paths, adding unnecessary hops and latency to reach game servers.
solutions: Run a traceroute to the game server to identify problematic hops
Contact your ISP with traceroute evidence of poor routing
Try a gaming VPN/tunneling service (ExitLag, WTFast) for optimized routes
Test during off-peak hours to see if routing improves
Consider a different ISP if routing is consistently poor

unstable-wifi.label: Unstable WiFi
unstable-wifi.description: WiFi connection keeps dropping or is unreliable

questions.text: How often does the WiFi disconnect?

options.label: Signal is weak but doesn't fully disconnect

questions.text: Does reconnecting fix the issue temporarily?
Are other devices also affected?

driver_issue.title: Network Adapter Driver Issue
driver_issue.description: Your WiFi adapter's driver is outdated, buggy, or incompatible, causing frequent disconnections.
solutions: Update your WiFi adapter driver from the manufacturer's website
Uninstall and reinstall the WiFi driver
Roll back to a previous driver version if a recent update caused issues
Check Device Manager for any warning icons on your network adapter
Try disabling and re-enabling the adapter in Device Manager

interference.title: Wireless Interference
interference.description: Other electronic devices or neighboring WiFi networks are interfering with your signal.
solutions: Use a WiFi analyzer to find the least congested channel
Switch from 2.4GHz to 5GHz band
Move the router away from microwaves, baby monitors, and Bluetooth devices
Reposition the router to a central, elevated location
Reduce the number of obstacles between you and the router

router_overload.title: Router Overloaded
router_overload.description: Your router is overwhelmed with too many connected devices or traffic, causing it to drop connections.
solutions: Restart your router to clear its memory
Check how many devices are connected and disconnect unused ones
Update your router's firmware
Reduce the maximum number of DHCP clients if configurable
Upgrade to a more capable router if yours is consistently overloaded

power_saving.title: WiFi Power Management
power_saving.description: Your device's power-saving features are turning off the WiFi adapter to conserve battery, causing disconnections.
solutions: Disable WiFi power saving: Device Manager → Network Adapter → Properties → Power Management → Uncheck 'Allow the computer to turn off this device'
Set your power plan to 'High Performance' when on AC power
Adjust wireless adapter power saving mode to 'Maximum Performance'
Disable sleep/hibernate if disconnections happen after idle periods
Keep your laptop plugged in during important network tasks

distance_obstacle.title: Distance / Obstacles
distance_obstacle.description: You are too far from the WiFi router, or walls/floors/objects are blocking the signal.
solutions: Move closer to the router or reposition the router centrally
Install a WiFi range extender or mesh system
Use a powerline adapter with WiFi for distant rooms
Remove or reduce obstacles between you and the router
Upgrade to a WiFi 6 router with better range and wall penetration
